text: We report a case of reversal omegaloop gastric bypass into normal anatomy by robotic approach in a patient who presented malnutrition after omegaloop gastric bypass, also called mini gastric bypass.
A sixty nine year old female who had undergone laparoscopic omegaloop gastric bypass in March, uh, twenty forty.
She had obstructive sleep apnea and type two diabetes.
In September, she had lost about a kilo with persistent diarrhea and a laboratory test showed hypoalbuminemia and anemia.
The weight loss curve is shown in this graph.
After a period of parenteral nutrition, she had improved her nutritional status.
We decided in a multidisciplinary staff to perform a reverse omega loop gastric bypass into normal anatomy using a robotic approach.

The surgical setup includes six trocars: a 12 mm port for the camera, two 8 mm ports for the robotic arms, two 12 mm ports for stapling and assistance, and an additional 5 mm port for liver retraction.
The robotic system is docked into position, preparing for the reverse omega loop gastric bypass procedure.
It was positioned at the patient left shoulder.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the left lobe of the liver, carefully freeing adhesions to expose the gastro-jejunostomy site.
The surgeon transects the gastrojejunostomy using a robotic stapler with purple staples, completing the division of the gastric and jejunal tissues.
The surgeon restores gastric continuity by performing a linear staple side-to-side anastomosis between the proximal gastric pouch and the excluded stomach, using surgical staplers.
An anisogastric tube is inserted through the gastric pouch into the excluded stomach to ensure the patency of the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the stapler entry hole using an unabsorbable barbed suture, ensuring the closure is secure and precise.
The surgeon resects the omega-loop jejunum and performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler to prevent intestinal stenosis.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a running, non-absorbable suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.

The surgeon assesses anastomotic leakage by injecting methylene blue-stained saline through an orogastric tube, observing the gastrogastric anastomosis for leaks. The anastomosis is then reinforced with biological glue.
The operative time was two hundred thirty-two minutes.
The contrast upper gastrointestinal study on the first postoperative day showed that the contrast drained well into the excluded stomach.
Post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged on day eight.
One month after the procedure, she has gained ten kilograms and stabilized nutritional status without further nutritional support.
